video_title: Chief Economist: Inflation Has Peaked — Here's What Happens To Bitcoin Next
gist: Wolf Research chief economist Stephanie Roth tells Anthony Pompliano that the latest CPI print came in flat versus a much hotter consensus, evidence that tariff-, AI/chip-shortage-, and Iran-war-driven inflation are all starting to peak and roll over. Despite the video title's Bitcoin framing, Bitcoin barely features — the substantive content is on Fed chair Kevin Warsh's communication overhaul, a resilient-but-unhappy consumer, shifting housing/work preferences, and why this year's mega-cap IPO wave (SpaceX, and reference to OpenAI/Anthropic staying private) doesn't look like a 1990s-style bubble; the 'debasement trade' into gold/Bitcoin is described as having already faded.

themes.theme: Inflation Peaking: Tariff, AI-Chip, and Energy Drivers Rolling Over

insights.claim: The latest CPI print came in flat month-over-month, far softer than consensus forecasts that had been calling for roughly 0.2% or higher, which took a potential July Fed hike nearly off the table.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: A much-softer-than-expected CPI print removes near-term hike risk and supports a 'peak inflation' narrative that argues for lower-for-longer rate expectations.
insights.quote: Today's CPI data came in much softer than anyone expected. It came in at flat.

insights.claim: Tariff-related inflation added an estimated 50-70 basis points to core goods prices (core goods ran above 2% year-over-year versus their typical disinflationary trend), but this effect is now peaking now that the economy is past the 'Liberation Day' tariff shock.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Tariff inflation was real but contained (not a feared inflationary spiral into services), and is a fading tailwind to headline CPI going forward.

insights.claim: Short-term AI-related inflation is showing up specifically through a chip shortage, with computer software and accessories components in the CPI basket running about 15% year-over-year, but this is expected to fade out of the data by the middle of the year rather than persist.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Investors should not extrapolate the current chip-shortage-driven inflation spike; it is a one-off base effect set to roll off mid-year, adding to the disinflation case.

insights.claim: Over the long run AI is expected to be disinflationary as productivity gains lower unit labor costs and let companies pass savings through to prices, even though near-term chip-shortage effects are temporarily inflationary.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Frames AI as a structural disinflationary force investors can weigh against near-term noisy inflation prints when thinking about the multi-year rate path.

insights.claim: Oil/energy inflation from the Iran war is expected to stay short-term and contained, with WTI likely settling in the $80s per barrel for a period; this mainly flows through into core inflation via airfares rather than broadly across the economy.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Even elevated oil prices in the $80s are framed as manageable for the consumer and not a broad-based inflation driver, limiting the case for a larger inflation resurgence.

insights.claim: Consumers are upset about the price LEVEL of goods (a step-change from post-pandemic stimulus and supply-chain shocks that companies rarely reverse), not the current rate of inflation, even though middle-to-upper income consumers can broadly still afford things because wage growth has largely kept pace outside of housing and autos.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Separates the psychological 'everything is expensive' narrative from actual purchasing power, explaining why consumer sentiment can stay depressed while spending data stays resilient.

themes.theme: The Warsh Fed: Communication Overhaul and Policy Path

insights.claim: New Fed chair Kevin Warsh has changed his communication style dramatically (moving away from forward guidance) even though actual policy has not changed much yet after only one meeting, making every incoming data print more market-moving because investors can no longer rely on the Fed telegraphing its path.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Expect higher volatility in rates and equities around every CPI/payrolls release since the market must now interpret data itself rather than lean on Fed guidance.

insights.claim: Stephanie Roth believes a rate cut is 'very far' away and unlikely this year; if anything, the more live near-term risk was a possible hike (e.g., at a September meeting) had recent payrolls and inflation data not moved in a favorable direction.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Pushes back against market narratives of imminent 2026 cuts; positions the realistic base case as the Fed holding rates steady through year-end absent labor-market weakness.
insights.quote: A cut is very far from now.

insights.claim: Anthony Pompliano floated a contrarian theory that the Warsh Fed and administration may believe AI-driven productivity gains and mass deportations are both structurally disinflationary forces, and could use that logic to justify cutting rates preemptively rather than waiting for hard labor-market weakness.
insights.speaker: host (Anthony Pompliano)
insights.so_what: If true, this would be a distinctly non-formulaic, forward-looking Fed reaction function investors should watch for versus the traditional 'data-dependent, react to weakness' Fed playbook.

insights.claim: Stephanie Roth argues that if the Fed does cut rates in 2026, it would signal real labor-market weakness rather than a policy victory, so the White House should not actually want a 2026 cut since current inflation (core running near 3%, ~100bps above target) doesn't justify one on its own.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Reframes a 2026 rate cut as a bearish signal (labor deterioration) rather than a bullish one, useful for interpreting market reaction if a cut does happen this year.
insights.quote: If there's a scenario where the Fed is cutting this year, I don't think the White House is going to be very happy with where the economy is.

insights.claim: The Fed will not officially change its 2% inflation target, but Roth and Pompliano debate whether it could unofficially tolerate a higher range without announcing it — Pompliano guesses 2.5-3%, Roth guesses closer to 2-2.5% — which would still be a meaningful shift from historical Fed behavior.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Watch whether the Fed cuts in late 2027 even with inflation still above 2% — that would be the tell for an unofficial, unannounced target shift.

insights.claim: A politically-motivated rate cut aimed at boosting markets ahead of the midterm election is seen by both speakers as an unlikely and self-defeating outcome, since it would be read as the Fed losing independence and raise the risk of a renewed inflation spiral, which would be negative for risk assets.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Investors shouldn't price in 'juice the market' cuts as a bullish catalyst — an unexpected, unjustified cut would more likely spook markets than rally them.
insights.quote: I think that would be very negative for markets.

insights.claim: Warsh's strategy is reportedly to establish Fed credibility and independence by leaning hawkish early, which brings down long-end yields (the 10-year) that actually anchor mortgage rates and business borrowing costs — a bigger lever than the federal funds rate itself.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Explains why a 'no cut, credible and hawkish' Fed posture can still be net-easing for the real economy via lower long-end rates, without an actual rate cut.
insights.quote: Nobody pays the federal funds rate, right? People pay the 10-year rate realistically.

themes.theme: Consumer Sentiment vs. Actual Spending: A Widening Gap

insights.claim: The stock market sits at all-time highs while consumer sentiment sits at all-time lows, an unusual divergence that both speakers flag as one of the more confusing signals in the current economy.
insights.speaker: host (Anthony Pompliano)
insights.so_what: A stock-market/sentiment divergence this wide suggests sentiment surveys may be overstating underlying economic distress relative to what asset prices and spending data show.
insights.quote: The stock market's at all time highs but consumer sentiment is at all-time lows.

insights.claim: Citing a Tom Lee report, Pompliano notes the Michigan Consumer Sentiment survey's political composition has shifted from roughly 50% Democrat/50% Republican historically to about two-thirds Democrat, one-third Republican, which mechanically skews the headline reading lower given the partisan gap in economic perception.
insights.speaker: host (Anthony Pompliano)
insights.so_what: Investors treating Michigan sentiment as a clean macro signal should adjust for this sampling shift before reading deep pessimism into the headline number.
insights.quote: They now are surveying 2/3 Democrats, one third Republican.

insights.claim: The headline consumer sentiment survey asks five questions, three of which relate to perceived affordability (e.g., is now a good time to buy durable goods, how do you feel about your finances) rather than actual purchase intent, so a negative reading doesn't necessarily predict reduced spending.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Sentiment surveys should be read as a gauge of how people feel about affordability, not as a leading indicator of actual consumption — credit card and bank data are the better spending signal.

insights.claim: Credit card spending data and bank earnings reports (as of the taping) show resilient, optimistic consumer spending trends that contradict the negative headline sentiment readings.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Hard spending data currently outweighs soft sentiment surveys as the more reliable read on consumer health for positioning purposes.

insights.claim: Roth believes U.S. government economic data (CPI, employment) is largely trustworthy and not politicized, but seasonal-adjustment models built on 5-8 year historical patterns struggle when underlying patterns shift (as they have post-pandemic), producing larger revisions and month-to-month volatility that make the data look less reliable than it is on a smoothed, year-over-year basis.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Investors should discount single-month data surprises and focus on smoothed/year-over-year trends rather than reading political motive into data volatility.

insights.claim: New York City rent hit a new all-time high in June, driven by concentrated demand for a limited supply of desirable housing rather than a broad national affordability collapse.
insights.speaker: host (Anthony Pompliano)
insights.so_what: Highlights that 'unaffordability' complaints are often concentrated in specific high-demand metros rather than reflecting the whole housing market.

insights.claim: A large share of perceived housing unaffordability is a mismatch between what people can actually afford (e.g., a home in a mid-tier suburb) and what they want (a specific big-city location or larger home), rather than true inability to buy any home.
insights.speaker: host (Anthony Pompliano)
insights.so_what: Suggests housing affordability narratives should distinguish genuine affordability crises from location/lifestyle preference, which matters for how policy or markets should respond.
insights.quote: There's actually a mismatch between what you can afford and what you want.

insights.claim: Elevated mortgage rates are keeping many current homeowners on the sidelines because they don't want to trade a lower locked-in rate for a materially higher one, a lock-in effect Roth expects to ease only gradually as rates come down modestly and incomes grow into current price levels.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: The housing market's supply constraint from rate lock-in is expected to unwind slowly, implying gradual rather than sharp improvement in transaction volumes and affordability.

insights.claim: A growing cohort, especially younger consumers and the FIRE community, increasingly prefers renting over owning because they've concluded homeownership is not as strong a financial investment as previously assumed, alongside a preference for job/location mobility.
insights.speaker: host (Anthony Pompliano)
insights.so_what: A structural shift in preference away from homeownership (not just affordability-driven) could keep rental demand and prices elevated even if mortgage rates ease.

insights.claim: Consumer spending preferences have shifted toward experiences and travel over physical goods in recent years, a trend both speakers expect to persist as younger generations place less value on traditional markers like homeownership.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Sector allocators should expect continued relative strength in travel/services spending versus goods retail as a generational trend, not just a temporary post-pandemic blip.

insights.claim: Employers are increasingly using workplace flexibility (remote/hybrid work) as a non-cash benefit to attract talent without raising pay, reflecting an evolving preference among younger workers for time efficiency and work-life balance over hours worked, even as large banks push for full return-to-office.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Companies offering flexibility can potentially manage labor costs and retention without raising wages, a relevant input for evaluating labor-cost trends and margins.

insights.claim: Earlier fears that AI would cause a sharp rise in unemployment have not materialized; instead the more likely path is gradual productivity gains, some unfilled job openings as AI absorbs certain tasks, and new job creation, ultimately lowering unit labor costs and reinforcing disinflation.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Investors worried about AI-driven labor market shocks should instead model a gradual productivity/disinflation story rather than a sudden unemployment spike.

insights.claim: Anthony Pompliano says he has completely reversed his prior view that AI would eliminate jobs, now believing it will create substantial new hiring demand for workers who know how to use the technology because it makes them more productive.
insights.speaker: host (Anthony Pompliano)
insights.so_what: A notable public reversal from a prominent commentator on AI-and-jobs, signaling the 'AI destroys jobs' narrative may be losing traction among market observers.
insights.quote: I have 100% changed my mind. I used to think it was going to take all the jobs. Now I think that it's going to create a ton of jobs.

insights.claim: AI productivity benefits have not yet shown up meaningfully in broad economic data because industry adoption rates remain low, but Roth expects this to change as adoption accelerates.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Productivity/margin gains from AI adoption are a forward-looking catalyst not yet priced into current macro data — worth tracking adoption-rate metrics by industry.

themes.theme: IPO Wave and Liquidity: Not the 1990s Bubble

insights.claim: Wolf Research's liquidity analysis finds that expected corporate buybacks this year should notably exceed the combined supply from new IPO issuance and expiring lockups, putting net demand in positive territory and suggesting the market can absorb the current IPO wave without a liquidity problem.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Counters the bearish 'too much IPO supply will overwhelm demand' narrative — buyback-driven demand is estimated to provide cushion for this year's issuance calendar.
insights.quote: This year corporate buyback should be pretty good.

insights.claim: Unlike past IPO waves that involved a broad breadth of companies going public, this year's wave is concentrated in a small handful of mega-cap IPOs (larger dollar issuance per deal), reflecting companies choosing to stay private longer and go public only when conditions were optimal, rather than a 1990s-style speculative frenzy.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Structurally different IPO wave composition (few mega-deals vs. broad speculative issuance) argues against directly analogizing this period to the dot-com bubble.

insights.claim: Current AI-related investment is only about 2% of GDP, well below the roughly 4% of GDP level historically associated with past major investment bubbles, indicating the AI investment boom has room to grow before reaching bubble-like scale, though malinvestment risk could emerge over the next couple of years.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Provides a concrete quantitative benchmark (2% vs. ~4% of GDP) for gauging how much further an AI capex boom could run before it resembles historical bubble scale.
insights.quote: In the past you've had big investment bubbles when said investment bubble was about 4% of GDP.

insights.claim: Companies like SpaceX are going public partly to access a different, larger quantum of capital than private markets offer and to gain broader investor engagement, with SpaceX's share price rising post-IPO and the company moving quickly into an acquisition, while other recent private-to-public valuation resets (Stripe's valuation coming down from its peak, Ramp raising at $6B after being valued near $10B before rising sharply since) show the public/private valuation gap being tested.
insights.speaker: host (Anthony Pompliano)
insights.so_what: Illustrates that going public is partly a price-discovery mechanism forcing formerly high-flying private valuations (like Stripe's) to reset against real market demand.

insights.claim: This year's favorable conditions for IPOs — high CEO confidence, lower volatility, higher valuations, and strong AI-driven growth narratives — mark a clear improvement from last year, when tariff-driven volatility discouraged companies from going public, but this does not by itself mean markets are at a bubble peak.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Frames the current IPO window as a cyclical opportunity created by favorable macro conditions rather than evidence of speculative excess, useful context for evaluating new listings.

themes.theme: Bitcoin/Gold Debasement Trade and Key Risks

insights.claim: The Fed-independence-risk 'debasement trade' into Bitcoin and gold, which was prominent roughly 9-12 months ago on fears of a non-independent Fed, has substantially cooled since Trump nominated Kevin Warsh, a candidate viewed as credible with a long, historically hawkish Fed track record, reducing fears of political capture.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: The macro narrative that drove flows into Bitcoin/gold as a Fed-independence hedge has largely unwound, meaning that catalyst is less likely to be a near-term driver of BTC/gold price action.

insights.claim: After briefly reigniting around Warsh's first press conference on fears the Fed would be more hawkish than expected, the debasement trade has now settled down, with markets refocusing on fundamentals, demand, and standard inflation dynamics rather than the non-independent-Fed narrative.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Suggests Bitcoin and gold price action going forward should be analyzed more through traditional supply/demand and inflation fundamentals rather than a Fed-debasement political narrative.
insights.quote: Now I think we're kind of settling in the middle where we're back to sort of trading on other things.

insights.claim: The top near-term risk keeping Roth up at night is the on-again, off-again uncertainty around the Iran war, which makes it hard for businesses and consumers to plan, echoing how last year's tariff-policy uncertainty (more than the tariffs' actual economic impact) was the bigger drag on sentiment and growth.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: Positions geopolitical/policy uncertainty itself (not the underlying economic fundamentals) as the swing factor most likely to slow the economy from here — worth monitoring headline flow on the conflict as a sentiment driver.
insights.quote: Nobody even knows if the war is on or off.

insights.claim: Aside from the Iran war uncertainty, Roth sees no other obvious domestic economic risks right now — a state of affairs she finds mildly concerning in itself, since an absence of visible risks typically means the eventual surprise will come from an unforeseen, likely non-domestic-economic source.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Roth, Wolf Research chief economist
insights.so_what: A 'no obvious risks' environment is itself a signal to stay alert for tail risk from an unexpected direction (geopolitical or otherwise) rather than complacency.
